hero_metrics.label: CIS GCP Foundations v2.0 controls passing
Public IPv4 listeners on compute tier

problem: I was running a homelab GCP environment with no compliance baseline and no way to know when it drifted. Firewall rules, IAM bindings, and audit-log sinks were configured by hand in the console — different each time, undocumented, and invisible to any scanner until something broke downstream. Out-of-band changes were the norm because nothing enforced the intended state. I needed three things: a CIS GCP Foundations Benchmark scan that runs before every change lands, a nightly drift-detection job that catches console modifications I didn't make, and a reconciliation path that reverts unauthorized changes automatically. The initial provisioning (VPC, NAT, IAM, firewall rules) had to be codified too — but provisioning was the starting state, not the point. The point was continuous compliance verification with Terraform as the enforcement mechanism.
constraints_in: Terraform as the only mutation path — no console changes accepted
CIS Google Cloud Foundations Benchmark v2.0 as the compliance target
GCS remote state backend (stay in GCP ecosystem, no additional SaaS bill)
Private-First network: zero public IPv4 on compute tier
All secrets via Secret Manager or Workload Identity Federation — never in .tfvars or git
CI-gated apply: no `terraform apply` from a laptop
constraints_out: Production-scale load testing (lab tested at 3 instances per tier)
Full SOC 2 or FedRAMP audit package
HashiCorp Vault integration (Secret Manager suffices at lab scale)
approach: The compliance loop has three stages: gate, apply, detect. Before any Terraform change merges, tfsec and Checkov scan the plan against CIS GCP Foundations Benchmark v2.0 — a HIGH finding blocks the PR. If the scan passes, a CI runner applies via Workload Identity Federation (short-lived OIDC credentials, no stored keys). After apply, a nightly cron runs terraform plan against the live state; any delta from the declared baseline triggers an auto-opened PR with the drift diff. The infrastructure itself is a modular Terraform registry (vpc-network, iam-service-accounts, gce-hardening, security-baselines, audit-sinks) producing a Private-First VPC — zero public IPs on compute, IAP tunnelling for admin access, Cloud NAT for controlled egress. State is in a GCS backend with native locking. No human runs terraform apply from a laptop; every mutation goes through the CI pipeline.

decisions.title: GCS remote state vs. Terraform Cloud vs. S3 + DynamoDB
options_considered: S3 + DynamoDB lock (AWS-hosted state for GCP infra)
GCS remote state with native locking
decisions.because: Keeps the state inside the same IAM boundary as the infrastructure being provisioned — a single trust model. No cross-cloud dependency for disaster recovery. No additional SaaS subscription. Native locking via GCS object versioning is battle-tested.
decisions.tradeoff: Lose Terraform Cloud's Sentinel policy UI and run history dashboard. Compensated by tfsec + Checkov in the CI pipeline and keeping the apply log as a GitHub Actions artifact.

decisions.title: Workload Identity Federation vs. long-lived JSON key for CI
options_considered: Long-lived JSON key stored in GitHub Secrets
JSON key in GCP Secret Manager pulled at run time
Workload Identity Federation (OIDC) with service account impersonation
decisions.because: Zero long-lived credentials exist anywhere — not on a laptop, not in GitHub Secrets, not in state. Every apply logs `gcloud iam workload-identity-pools describe` evidence of who triggered the impersonation. Matches the CIS control for credential lifetime and the NIST AC-2 / IA-5 intent.
decisions.tradeoff: One-time setup cost for the pool, provider, and IAM binding. ~90 minutes of YAML and gcloud calls versus the 10 minutes a JSON key would have taken.

decisions.title: Private-First VPC with IAP tunnel vs. bastion host vs. hybrid public
options_considered: Hybrid public (some tiers have public IPs)
Private-First + IAP TCP tunnel for admin access
decisions.because: Zero public inbound listeners. No bastion to patch. IAP integrates with IAM and audit logs so every admin session is attributable. Satisfies CIS 3.1 (no public SSH) and the NIST AC-4 boundary-protection intent without running additional infrastructure.
decisions.tradeoff: IAP has a per-byte cost. Acceptable at lab scale (~$0.50/month observed). At production scale would require a cost model.

decisions.title: Module registry vs. flat main.tf vs. Terragrunt
options_considered: In-repo module registry with semantic versioning
Terragrunt for DRY wrapper over modules
decisions.chose: In-repo module registry with semver tagging
decisions.because: Blast radius is scoped per-module — a breaking change in vpc-network does not force re-plan of the iam module. Each module has explicit inputs/outputs and its own README. No additional tool to learn for the next operator.
decisions.tradeoff: More files to navigate. Mitigated with a consistent layout and a top-level README.md that diagrams the module dependency tree.

highlights.why: Backend config binds three things that must be consistent: the GCS bucket for state, the prefix path that segments environments, and the impersonated service account that executes the apply. No `google_credentials` variable, no JSON file path — the CI runner is assumed to have already federated via OIDC. Any plan from a laptop fails with a credentials error, which is the desired behavior.

`auto_create_subnetworks = false` is the Private-First hinge — GCP's default creates 20+ regional subnets with public-reachable defaults; turning it off means you must explicitly opt in to every subnet, every route, every peering. Cloud NAT + error-only logging captures the audit trail required for CIS 3.5 without flooding logs with every successful egress.

highlights.code:
jobs:
  security-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: aquasecurity/tfsec-action@v1.0.3
        with:
          working_directory: terraform/
          additional_args: --config-file .tfsec/config.yml --soft-fail=false
      - uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@v12
        with:
          directory: terraform/
          framework: terraform
          soft_fail: false

  plan:
    needs: security-gate
    # ... OIDC auth + terraform plan
highlights.why: The security gate runs BEFORE the plan job — meaning a CIS violation fails the pipeline before any credential is federated or any plan is computed. The `soft_fail: false` is explicit: a tfsec HIGH finding blocks the merge. Without this, compliance is an afterthought; with this, it is a prerequisite.

outcomes.baseline: ~4 hours manual console click-through (VPC + IAM + firewall + NAT + monitoring)
outcomes.method: 10 consecutive `terraform apply -auto-approve` runs against a freshly created empty GCP project, measured with `time` wrapper on a GitHub Actions ubuntu-latest runner. Mean 4:32, stdev 0:18, range 4:11 – 5:07.

outcomes.baseline: Blind — no detection until an unrelated failure surfaced the drift
outcomes.result: Detected on next scheduled `terraform plan` (daily cron) or on next PR
outcomes.method: Manually introduced drift via GCP Console (added an unauthorized allow-all-tcp firewall rule). Nightly plan run detected the extra resource as a deletion in the next apply plan. Detection latency ≤ 24 h by design of the schedule.

outcomes.metric: CIS GCP Foundations Benchmark v2.0 coverage
outcomes.baseline: Unknown — no scan had ever run
outcomes.method: `tfsec --config-file .tfsec/config.yml terraform/` and `checkov -d terraform/ --framework terraform`. Full report in reports/cis-scan-2026-04-15.txt. The 5 failing controls are documented accepted-tradeoffs in the Limitations section of the Docs page.

outcomes.metric: Plaintext credentials in the repository history
outcomes.baseline: Provider JSON key committed to early commits (removed, but in history)
outcomes.result: Zero after git-filter-repo rewrite + Workload Identity migration
outcomes.method: `git log --all -p | grep -iE 'private_key|BEGIN.*PRIVATE KEY|password|api_key'` = no matches. `trufflehog git file://. --only-verified` clean. State inspection via `terraform state pull | jq` reveals no plaintext keys.

lessons: I started with a single flat main.tf and hit the blast-radius problem within two weeks. A one-character typo in a firewall rule forced a full re-plan of the entire stack (~90 s) and made code review a nightmare. Moving to the module registry pattern cut the feedback loop on individual changes from ~90 s to ~8 s per module. Next time, I start modular day one, even if it feels like over-engineering on day one.

tfsec and Checkov disagree on rule severity roughly 15% of the time. Canonicalizing on tfsec as the CI gate and using Checkov as a secondary advisory was the pragmatic compromise — but I wasted a full evening trying to reconcile them before making that call. Tool standardization over coverage-breadth is the right default.

I underestimated the IAP tunneling setup cost. A Private-First network is cheap to describe; operationally reaching it for admin work is not. If I did this again I would spike the admin-access pattern (IAP tunnel from laptop, credential flow, IAM bindings) in a scratch project BEFORE committing to Private-First for the real thing. The cost is the unfamiliarity, not the dollars.
